title: Privacy Extension Address Churn Tracking
dataSources: `index=network sourcetype=ndp:cache` from NDP cache polling (SNMP `ipv6NetToMediaTable` or CLI `show ipv6 neighbors`), or `index=netflow sourcetype=ipfix` for passively observed IPv6 source addresses, or `index=dhcp sourcetype=infoblox:dhcp` for DHCPv6 lease churn. Key fields: `ipv6_address`, `mac_address`, `_time`. The critical observation is the number of distinct IPv6 addresses per MAC address over a 24-hour period. RFC 8981 temporary addresses have a default preferred lifetime of 24 hours and generate a new address daily. With privacy extensions enabled, a typical host will use 2-5 addresses simultaneously (1 stable + 1-4 temporary) and cycle through 3-8 unique addresses per day.
app: Splunk Connect for SNMP (SC4SNMP) or SNMP Modular Input for NDP cache polling, Splunk Add-on for NetFlow for flow-based discovery, Cisco Networks Add-on (`TA-cisco_ios`, Splunkbase 1352) for syslog.
spl:
index=network sourcetype="ndp:cache" earliest=-24h
| where NOT match(ipv6_address, "^fe80:")
| stats dc(ipv6_address) as addr_count values(ipv6_address) as addresses latest(_time) as last_seen by mac_address, host
| eval churn_level=case(
    addr_count > 20, "EXCESSIVE — possible misconfiguration or scanning",
    addr_count > 10, "HIGH — very short temporary address lifetime",
    addr_count > 5, "NORMAL — privacy extensions active",
    addr_count > 1, "LOW — stable + few temporary",
    1==1, "MINIMAL — likely static or SLAAC without privacy")
| where addr_count > 10
| eval last_seen=strftime(last_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| sort -addr_count
description: Tracks the number of distinct IPv6 addresses generated by each device (identified by MAC address) over a 24-hour period to detect excessive address churn caused by RFC 8981 privacy extension temporary addresses. Privacy extensions are enabled by default on all modern operating systems (Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android) to prevent activity tracking by hiding the stable SLAAC address behind short-lived temporary addresses. While beneficial for privacy, excessive churn (> 20 addresses/day/device) can indicate misconfigured preferred lifetime settings, exhaust NDP caches on switches, break log correlation, and make forensic investigation difficult.
value: Privacy extensions create a fundamental tension between user privacy and network accountability. RFC 9099 §2.6.1.5 acknowledges this tension and recommends monitoring address churn to balance both concerns. From an operations perspective, excessive churn wastes NDP cache entries (each temporary address consumes a cache slot), strains DHCPv6 servers (if stateful), and makes it harder to correlate a user's activity across log sources. From a security perspective, a single device generating > 50 addresses/day may be running a scanning tool or malware that deliberately creates new addresses to evade detection. This UC distinguishes normal privacy extension behaviour (3-8 addresses/day) from anomalous behaviour (> 20 addresses/day) and identifies the specific devices involved.
implementation: Poll NDP caches from switches via SNMP every 5-10 minutes. The search counts unique IPv6 addresses per MAC over 24 hours and flags devices exceeding the threshold. This requires NDP cache data (Layer 2+3 binding) rather than flow data (Layer 3 only), because flow data cannot correlate multiple IPv6 addresses to the same physical device. If NDP polling isn't available, the search can use flow data with a less precise heuristic (same /64 prefix + similar access port = likely same device).
visualization: (1) Histogram: distribution of address count per device (most devices should cluster at 1-8, outliers at > 20 need investigation). (2) Table: devices with > 10 addresses/day — MAC, address count, churn level, sample addresses, last seen. (3) Timechart: average address-per-device ratio over 30 days — trend should be stable unless privacy extension defaults change (e.g., OS update reducing preferred lifetime). (4) Single-value: count of devices with excessive churn (> 20 addresses/day).

references.title: RFC 8981 — Temporary Address Extensions for Stateless Address Autoconfiguration in IPv6 (privacy extensions)
RFC 9099 — Operational Security Considerations for IPv6 Networks (§2.6.1.5 — Address accountability vs privacy)
RFC 7721 — Security and Privacy Considerations for IPv6 Address Generation Mechanisms

knownFalsePositives:
**Servers with many virtual interfaces.** A server running containers (Docker, Kubernetes) or VMs may have many IPv6 addresses legitimately — one per container/VM. These all share the host's physical MAC but have distinct IPv6 addresses. Distinguish by correlating with the server inventory — known container hosts should be excluded or have a higher threshold.

**Mobile devices reconnecting to different APs.** When a device roams between WiFi access points on the same VLAN, it may generate a new temporary address on reconnect (depending on OS implementation). If roaming is frequent (every 15-30 minutes in a large campus), the address count per day can be high. The MAC address stays the same, so the correlation is correct.

**OS updates changing privacy extension behaviour.** A Windows or macOS update may change the default preferred lifetime for temporary addresses, causing a fleet-wide shift in churn rates. This isn't a per-device anomaly — it's a baseline shift. Check if the churn increase correlates with a recent OS patch Tuesday.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• NDP cache polling from access/distribution switches (see UC-5.20.4 prerequisites for SNMP `ipv6NetToMediaTable` setup). Poll interval of 5 minutes or shorter is ideal — temporary addresses can be short-lived, and infrequent polling will miss some addresses.
• Understanding of RFC 8981 defaults by operating system:
  - Windows 10/11: temporary addresses enabled by default, preferred lifetime 24h, valid lifetime 7d. Generates ~3-5 addresses/day.
  - macOS: temporary addresses enabled, preferred lifetime 24h. Generates ~3-5 addresses/day.
  - Linux (NetworkManager): depends on distro; most enable privacy by default. `sysctl net.ipv6.conf.all.use_tempaddr` — 0=disabled, 1=enabled-prefer-public, 2=enabled-prefer-temporary.
  - iOS/Android: temporary addresses enabled, very aggressive churn (~8-12 addresses/day) to defeat WiFi tracking.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same as UC-5.20.4 — NDP cache polling via SNMP or scripted CLI. Ensure the poll interval is ≤ 5 minutes for accurate address churn measurement.

Verification:
```spl
index=network sourcetype="ndp:cache" earliest=-24h
| where NOT match(ipv6_address, "^fe80:")
| stats dc(ipv6_address) as addr_count by mac_address
| stats avg(addr_count) as avg_addrs_per_mac max(addr_count) as max_addrs_per_mac dc(mac_address) as total_macs
```
Expected: `avg_addrs_per_mac` should be 2-8 in a network with privacy extensions enabled. If it's 1, either privacy extensions are disabled fleet-wide or the polling interval is too long to capture temporary addresses before they expire.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

**Primary search — privacy extension churn analysis:**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="ndp:cache" earliest=-24h
| where NOT match(ipv6_address, "^fe80:")
| stats dc(ipv6_address) as addr_count values(ipv6_address) as addresses latest(_time) as last_seen latest(host) as last_switch latest(interface) as last_port by mac_address
| eval churn_level=case(
    addr_count > 50, "CRITICAL — scanning tool or malware suspected",
    addr_count > 20, "EXCESSIVE — misconfigured privacy lifetime or container host",
    addr_count > 10, "HIGH — very short temporary lifetime",
    addr_count > 5, "NORMAL — privacy extensions active",
    addr_count > 1, "LOW — stable + few temporary",
    1==1, "MINIMAL — static or no privacy extensions")
| eval last_seen=strftime(last_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| sort -addr_count
```

**Understanding this SPL:**
- Groups by `mac_address` to correlate multiple IPv6 addresses to a single physical device. This is the only reliable correlation — IPv6 addresses alone can't identify a device when privacy extensions are active.
- `dc(ipv6_address)` counts distinct addresses per MAC over 24 hours. This is the churn metric.
- `values(ipv6_address)` lists all addresses for inspection — useful for determining whether the addresses are SLAAC-derived (contain the /64 prefix from the RA) or from other sources.
- Filters out link-local (fe80::) because every device always has exactly one link-local address derived from its MAC — this doesn't churn.

**Alert — excessive churn (potential scanning or misconfiguration):**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="ndp:cache" earliest=-24h
| where NOT match(ipv6_address, "^fe80:")
| stats dc(ipv6_address) as addr_count by mac_address
| where addr_count > 20
| lookup asset_inventory mac_address OUTPUT hostname, device_type
| table mac_address, hostname, device_type, addr_count
```
Trigger: any device with > 20 distinct IPv6 addresses in 24 hours. Exclude known container hosts via lookup.

**NDP cache impact assessment:**
```spl
index=network sourcetype="ndp:cache" earliest=-24h
| stats dc(ipv6_address) as cache_entries by host
| lookup ndp_cache_limits host OUTPUT max_cache_size
| eval utilisation_pct=round(cache_entries / max_cache_size * 100, 1)
| where utilisation_pct > 70
| sort -utilisation_pct
```
This shows switches where privacy extension churn is consuming NDP cache capacity. NDP cache exhaustion can cause packet drops for all IPv6 traffic on the switch.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Spot-check a known device:** On your own laptop, run `ip -6 addr show` (Linux) or `ipconfig /all` (Windows). Count the non-link-local addresses. This should approximately match the `addr_count` in Splunk for your MAC address.

(b) **Platform baseline:** Run the churn analysis grouped by device type (if you have a device type lookup). Windows devices should show 3-5, iOS/Android 8-12, Linux varies by configuration.

(c) **Verify high-churn outliers:** For any device with > 20 addresses, investigate: is it a container host? A server with many vNICs? A device running a scanning tool? Or genuinely misconfigured privacy extensions?

Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard** (panel on "IPv6 — Address Management"):
• Row 1 — Histogram: address count distribution (log scale). Most devices cluster at 1-8. Outliers on the right need investigation.
• Row 2 — Table: devices with > 10 addresses/day.
• Row 3 — Single-value: average addresses per device (trend over time).

**Scheduling:** Daily analysis over `-24h`. Alert for > 20 threshold.

**Runbook:**
1. High churn (10-20 addresses/day): likely aggressive privacy extension settings. If this is fleet-wide after an OS update, update the baseline. If isolated to one device, check its IPv6 privacy configuration.
2. Excessive churn (> 20): investigate the device. Check for scanning tools (nmap, masscan), malware using address rotation for evasion, or Docker/K8s containers creating addresses.
3. NDP cache impact: if switch cache utilisation > 80% due to churn, consider: increasing NDP cache size (`ipv6 nd cache interface-limit` on Cisco), or reducing temporary address lifetime on clients via RA options (enterprise MDM can push this policy).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **All devices show addr_count = 1** — Privacy extensions may be disabled, OR the NDP polling interval is too long (> 24 hours, so temporary addresses expire between polls). Reduce poll interval to ≤ 5 minutes.

• **Very high addr_count (> 100) for a single MAC** — Almost certainly a container host or VM hypervisor. Verify with your asset inventory.

• **addr_count varies wildly day to day for the same device** — The device may not be continuously connected (laptop that's only on the network 8 hours/day). Normalise by dividing addr_count by hours-on-network.

• **Cannot correlate MAC to user** — This is the intended effect of privacy extensions. For accountability, use 802.1X/RADIUS authentication (which logs MAC-to-user binding) combined with NDP cache data. See UC-5.20.15 for the full forensic correlation chain.
grandmaExplanation: We count how many different internet addresses each device uses throughout the day. Modern devices deliberately change their address frequently for privacy — like using different pen names. We make sure nobody is changing addresses way too fast, which would suggest either a computer problem or someone trying to hide suspicious activity.
